actions.thought: 8-10 offsuit is a marginal hand that lacks significant high-card strength or connectivity. As this is the first hand of the tournament, I prefer to play a disciplined strategy, conserving my stack and observing my opponents' tendencies before engaging in pots. Folding is the most prudent and low-risk decision here.
92o is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite the small cost to call, it has terrible playability post-flop and negative expected value. I will fold and save my chips for a better spot.

On the button in a 3-handed game, T8o is a bit too loose to open-raise. Although stealing blinds is important, offsuit gappers play poorly post-flop and face a significant risk of being 3-bet. Folding is the safest and most standard play to preserve chips for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind holding 7s 2d, which is widely considered the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. With the Button folding, I am only facing the Big Blind. While I could attempt to steal the blinds with a raise or complete the bet for 50 more, 72o has extremely poor playability and equity, especially when I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. In a 150-hand tournament, there is no reason to force a play with the absolute bottom of my range. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 4s 5c, which is a very weak hand even in a three-handed game. While the button offers a positional advantage, 45 offsuit has very little equity and does not play well post-flop. Given the early stage of the tournament, I will opt for a disciplined fold rather than trying to steal the blinds with such a marginal hand.

With 7s6s in the small blind against the big blind after the button folded, I am in a favorable heads-up spot. Given the deep stacks (100BB), 7s6s is a highly playable suited connector. Raising to 250 allows me to leverage my position (though technically out of position post-flop, I have the betting lead) and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or set up a post-flop continuation bet. This is a standard aggressive opening range play in this specific heads-up scenario.

My hand is 53 offsuit, which is completely trash. Facing a raise from the small blind to 2.5bb, I do not have enough equity or playability to justify defending, even with position. Folding is the only sensible play here.

7d6d is a strong suited connector, perfect for an opening raise from the button in a short-handed game. By raising to 300, I can take control of the hand, potentially steal the blinds, or define my range while leveraging my positional advantage.

In a 3-handed game, the Button opens to 3 big blinds (300). I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Eight suited (Ts8s). While it has some post-flop playability, calling out of position with a marginal suited gapper against a 3x open leaves me vulnerable to a squeeze from the Big Blind and is difficult to play profitably post-flop. A 3-bet bluff is an option, but folding is the most solid, standard decision to preserve my stack early in the match.

I have a strong pocket pair (88) and am facing a standard button open. 88 is well ahead of a typical button opening range. I will 3-bet to 1000 to take control of the pot and build it while I likely have the best hand, despite being out of position for the rest of the hand.

My opening raise to 300 was 3-bet to 1000 by the big blind. 7d6d is a strong suited connector that performs well in position. With effective stacks deep enough (roughly 90BB behind), folding is too tight given the pot odds, and 4-betting is too high-variance for this specific hand. I will call the 700 to see the flop in position and realize my equity.

The flop of Ad-Tc-5s is quite unfavorable for my pocket eights, as it hits many of the hands that the Button would call a 3-bet with, such as Ax and Tx. Being out of position, I should check to see how the opponent reacts. Betting into an Ace-high board where the opponent likely has an Ace or a Ten is low-value and high-risk. I will check and evaluate based on the action.

The flop of 5s Ad Tc completely misses my 7d 6d hand. The opponent, who 3-bet pre-flop, checked to me. This board texture heavily favors the 3-bettor's range as it contains many combinations of high pairs and strong Ace-high hands. Attempting a continuation bet here would be turning a hand with zero equity into a bluff against a range that is likely to call or check-raise. Checking back is the most prudent play to control the pot size and take a free turn card, hoping to realize some equity if I connect with the turn.

The turn pairs the Ace (As), making the board 5s Ad Tc As. On the flop, both of us checked. My check on an Ace-high flop as the 3-bettor usually indicates a hand like a medium pocket pair (which I have) or a missed broadway. Flashlight's check back on the flop suggests he likely doesn't have a strong Ace or a Ten, or he is playing very cautiously. The second Ace makes it even less likely he has one. However, 88 is still a marginal hand here. Betting is unlikely to get value from worse hands (except maybe 66-77) and might get called or raised by better hands like a Ten. I will check to maintain pot control and reach showdown cheaply, as my hand has significant showdown value against his likely range of air or small pairs.

The board of 5s Ad Tc As is extremely dry and heavily favors the 3-bettor's range, which likely includes many Ace-high hands, strong pairs, or broadway cards. My 7d 6d has zero showdown value and no realistic equity against that range. Since the opponent checked to me, betting is a pure bluff that would likely be called by even the weakest part of their range. Checking back is the most disciplined play to minimize losses, take a free river card, and maintain control of the pot size.

The river is a blank 3h. Both players have checked the flop and turn, which suggests that neither player has a strong hand like an Ace or a set. My pocket eights have significant showdown value against Flashlight's range, which likely consists of missed broadway cards or middle-to-low pairs. Betting now won't get value from worse hands (except perhaps 66 or 77) and might get called by a Ten that was checking back. I will check to see the showdown and hopefully win the pot with my pair.

I hold 7d 6d on a board of 5s Ad Tc As 3h. I have no pair and zero showdown value. The opponent checked to me on the river. Betting would be a pure bluff, and given their pre-flop 3-bet, they likely hold an Ace or high-card strength that will not fold. Checking behind is the most prudent strategy to reach showdown without further investing in a losing hand.

K8o on the button in a 3-handed game is a marginal hand. Given we are 100BB deep and opponents have shown aggression, it's better to fold this weak offsuit king and wait for better spots rather than risk playing a dominated hand out of position post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in a blind-vs-blind scenario. Since the Button has folded, I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and build a pot with a hand that likely dominates much of the Big Blind's defending range.

Ah, Td is a strong holding in a blind-versus-blind situation. Since the small blind opened the action, their range is likely wide, and my hand plays well against that range. By 3-betting, I can take the lead, put pressure on the opponent, and potentially win the pot immediately or maintain initiative post-flop. A raise to 900 is an appropriate sizing to define the strength of my hand and build the pot.

I raised from the Small Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit, and the Big Blind has 3-bet to 900. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, Ace-Ten is too strong to fold in a blind-vs-blind battle. Calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity without over-committing to a massive pot pre-flop against a potentially stronger range. I am getting 2-to-1 odds on the call.

I have middle pair (Tens) with an Ace kicker on a Queen-high board. Since I called a 3-bet pre-flop and am acting first (out of position), the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows me to see how they react to the board texture. My hand is relatively strong but vulnerable, so checking helps control the size of the pot and gives me the opportunity to call a continuation bet if they are bluffing or betting a draw.

I hold middle pair (Tens) with an Ace kicker on a coordinated board (6h-Qc-Tc). Given that I 3-bet pre-flop, I should maintain initiative here. Betting 1/3 of the pot (600) serves to protect my hand against potential flush or straight draws, while also gaining value from weaker pairs or float attempts from the small blind. Checking would allow a free card, which is suboptimal given the board texture.

I have middle pair (Tens) with the best kicker (Ace) on a board of Qc-Tc-6h. My opponent 3-bet pre-flop and is now making a continuation bet of 600 into a 1800-chip pot (1/3 pot). Given the strength of my hand and the excellent pot odds (calling 600 to win 2400), folding would be too tight. Raising is unnecessary as it would likely isolate me against better hands like Queens or overpairs. Calling allows me to control the pot size, evaluate the turn, and keep my opponent's bluffs or weaker draws in the hand.

The King of Diamonds on the turn is a significant scare card as it hits many hands in a standard 3-betting range, such as AK, KQ, or even KK. My pair of Tens is now third pair on a very coordinated board. Being out of position, I will check to the aggressor to control the pot and evaluate their action. Betting here would likely only be called by hands that beat me.

The board has become significantly more dangerous with the King on the turn, as it likely interacts well with the opponent's calling range (KQ, KJ, or K-x hands). My pair of Tens has limited strength and is now effectively a bluff-catcher. Betting into the opponent again would likely force me to fold to a raise or commit chips against hands that have me beat. By checking back, I control the pot size and look to get to showdown or re-evaluate on the river without bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

On the river board of 6h-Qc-Tc-Kd-6s, I have two pair (Tens and Sixes) with an Ace kicker. The turn King was a scare card that slowed down the action, as Flashlight checked back. This suggests Flashlight might have a hand with some showdown value that didn't want to bloat the pot, or a missed draw. By checking the river, I am playing pot control and allowing Flashlight to bluff with missed draws (like clubs or AJ) or simply take a free showdown. Betting myself is unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I would be forced to fold to a significant raise. Checking is the most prudent way to realize my hand's value.

On this board (6h-Qc-Tc-Kd-6s), my hand of Ah-Td gives me two pair (Tens and Sixes). However, any hand containing a King or a Queen also pairs the board or beats me with a higher pair. Given that the opponent has checked both the turn and the river, it is likely they are either holding a marginal hand, a missed draw, or potentially a King/Queen they are afraid to bet. Since betting would only be called by hands that beat me (like a King or Queen) and would fold out hands that I already beat, checking back is the optimal play to reach a showdown and realize my equity safely.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with Jack-Eight offsuit. While J8o is a marginal hand, in a three-handed environment, it is typically strong enough to open for a raise to put pressure on the blinds and attempt to take the pot pre-flop. Given the stack sizes and the behavior observed in previous hands, a standard 2.5x raise is a solid play.

I am in the small blind with suited connectors (Qc9c) facing a standard raise from the button. Calling is a reasonable defensive play here; while I will be out of position, the hand has good post-flop playability and implied odds, and folding would be too tight given the pot odds.

I am in the Big Blind with an extremely weak, offsuit, disconnected hand (5c 8h). The Button raised to 250 and the Small Blind called. I'm facing a raise and a call out of position. Even with attractive pot odds, playing this trash hand post-flop against two opponents is a losing play. Folding is the easiest and most sensible choice.

The board texture is highly coordinated (A-K-Q) and significantly favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Holding middle pair with no kicker, I am in a precarious position against a range that likely includes many Aces, Kings, and better Queens, or straight draws like J-T. Given I am out of position, checking is the most prudent action to control the pot size and re-evaluate based on the opponent's subsequent actions.

The flop of Ah-Kd-Qd is extremely coordinated and likely hits my opponent's range after they called my pre-flop raise. While I have a gutshot straight draw with the Jack of Diamonds and a backdoor diamond flush draw, betting into this board is risky as the opponent is unlikely to fold any piece of it. Checking back allows me to see a free turn card, control the pot size, and potentially realize my equity if a Ten peels off.

The board is highly coordinated with A-K-Q, making my pair of Queens very vulnerable. My opponent checked the flop behind me, which suggests they likely don't have a monster hand, but they could still have a weak Ace, a King, or a straight draw. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands or cause folds from bluffs that I currently beat. Given my lack of position and the strength of the board, checking is the most disciplined way to control the pot size and keep my hand as a bluff catcher.

The board (AKQ3) is extremely heavy with Broadway cards that likely connect with Flashlight's calling range. I have Jack-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing any Ten). Since Flashlight has checked both the flop and the turn, they are showing weakness, but my hand has zero showdown value. By checking behind, I take a free card to try and hit my straight on the river without risking more chips, while maintaining the option to bluff on the river if the situation looks profitable.

The board is A-K-Q-3-7. My pair of queens is a marginal hand that is likely only good as a bluff catcher. The opponent checked behind on both the flop and turn, which indicates they likely do not have a strong Ace or King, but they could still be trapping or holding a small pair that I currently beat. Betting would only isolate me against hands that beat me (if I get called) or fold out hands that I already beat. Checking is the most prudent strategy to control the pot size and reach a showdown affordably.

On a board of Ad-Kh-Qd-3h-7d, my Jack-high has virtually zero showdown value. Flashlight has checked every street, which suggests weakness or a marginal hand like a weak Queen or King that he's trying to get to showdown cheaply. While the 7d completed a potential diamond flush and I hold the Jd blocker, the board is so heavily connected with the broadway cards that likely make up Flashlight's calling range from the small blind that a bluff is unlikely to succeed against any pair. I will check behind and concede the pot to preserve my stack.

The hand 73o is objectively one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. It lacks equity, high-card strength, and board coverage. Entering the pot with this holding, even from the button position, is a negative expected value (-EV) play. I will fold and conserve my stack for a more profitable opportunity.

I have pocket Aces (Ac As) in the Small Blind and the action has folded to me. I want to build the pot and extract value from the Big Blind. A standard raise size from the small blind in this situation is 3x the big blind, so I will raise to 300.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Three offsuit. The Small Blind has raised to 300. While I have position post-flop in this heads-up situation, Q3o is a very weak hand that does not realize its equity well. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve chips for better opportunities.

I am on the Button with T4o, a very weak offsuit hand. It lacks high card strength, suitedness, and connectivity. Since the blinds are still small and I have a deep stack, the best play is to simply fold and wait for a more profitable spot.
In a blind-on-blind scenario where the button has folded, Ace-Nine offsuit is a premium hand. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and extract value from the big blind's wide defending range.

Flash opened from the small blind with a 3x raise. With Ace-Five offsuit in the big blind, this is a standard defense situation. My hand is strong enough to call and see a flop with a decent Ace, and I have a positional advantage post-flop. The pot odds of 2:1 are favorable to make the call.

On a board of 4c-Jh-Td, my Ace-high has some showdown value but is vulnerable. This texture interacts well with the Big Blind's defending range, which includes many Jx, Tx, and straight draws. By checking, I control the pot size and can evaluate Flashlight's action. If they check back, I get a free turn card to potentially improve or reach showdown. If they bet, I can decide based on the sizing whether to continue or fold.

The flop is relatively dry with a Jack and a Ten, and I have only Ace-high with a backdoor flush draw. Flash checked to me, and since I am in position, checking back allows me to see the turn for free, control the pot size, and re-evaluate if I pick up more equity on the next card. Betting here would likely be a pure bluff into a board texture that is likely to have connected with the opponent's raising range.

The turn card (2h) is a complete blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture, though it does introduce a backdoor heart draw. After the flop went check-check, my Ace-high (As 9c) still holds considerable showdown value against Flashlight's wide defending range. Betting here doesn't achieve much as I'm unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I don't want to get raised off my equity. By checking, I continue to control the pot size and can potentially induce bluffs or reach a cheap showdown. If Flashlight checks back, I am likely ahead.

With Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw on a board of 4c Jh Td 2h, I have some showdown value but limited equity. Flash has checked to me twice now, which likely indicates either a missed hand or a trap. Betting here is unlikely to get better hands to fold, and I have enough equity to see a river card for free. Checking back is the most balanced and strategic decision to control the pot size and realize my equity.

On a board of 4c-Jh-Td-2h-6h, the action has gone check-check on both the flop and the turn. My Ace-high (As 9c) has significant showdown value in this blind-vs-blind scenario, as Flashlight's checks suggest a weak hand or a missed draw. Betting now is unlikely to get called by worse hands or fold out better ones (like a weak Jack or Ten). By checking, I can reach showdown for free and likely win against King-high, Queen-high, or lower Ace-high hands. If Flashlight bets, I will have to evaluate based on the sizing, but checking is the best way to realize my hand's equity.

I have Ace-high on a board where many cards like the Jack, Ten, Six, or lower pairs could have easily hit my opponent's range. Betting here has very little value as I will only be called by hands that beat me and will fold out hands I currently beat. Checking behind allows me to realize my showdown value and avoid inflating a pot where I am likely behind, while also being the most cautious approach given the lack of strength in my hand.
Pocket nines is a premium hand in 3-handed play. Being on the button, I want to exert pressure and build a pot. Raising to 3x the big blind is a standard opening size.

7-2 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Playing this hand out of position from the small blind against a button open is mathematically incorrect and would lead to difficult post-flop spots. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play to preserve stack health.

We are in the Big Blind with 8h 6s, which is a weak offsuit one-gapper. The Button (Flash) raised to 3 big blinds. Calling out of position with this hand is unprofitable, and we don't have a strong enough holding to 3-bet bluff profitably against an average button opening range. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game. 9-8 offsuit is a playable hand in this position to attempt to steal the blinds. Given that my opponents have been playing passively and checking down many streets, taking the initiative pre-flop is a reasonable strategic move to potentially take down the pot early.

QJo is a marginal offsuit broadway hand. Facing a button raise, playing from the small blind is generally a tricky situation due to being out of position post-flop and the risk of the big blind squeezing. Folding is the safest and most standard play here to preserve chips and avoid marginal spots.

I am holding a pocket pair (5c, 5s) in the Big Blind. The Button (Flashlight) has opened for 2.5x. This is a standard defending situation. I have great pot odds to call and try to set-mine, and pocket fives have decent raw equity against a button opening range. I will call to close the pre-flop action and see a flop.

I have pocket fives on a dry board with one overcard (Queen) and one middle card (Seven). As the player who defended the big blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Donk betting here doesn't accomplish much against a standard button range. Checking allows me to evaluate Flashlight's continuation bet and potentially see a turn for free if they check back.

I am the pre-flop aggressor on a dry board (Q-high). The opponent checked to me, which often signals weakness or a marginal hand. A continuation bet of 250 into the 550 pot is a standard move to represent strength and take down the pot right here, as it's unlikely the opponent connected strongly with this specific board texture.

The flop of 2c-Qh-7s is relatively dry. While there is a Queen on the board, my pocket fives are likely ahead of a significant portion of Flashlight's button opening range, which includes many Ace-high and King-high hands. Flashlight's continuation bet of 250 into 550 is a standard sizing that I should not fold to, especially since I am getting over 3:1 pot odds. I will call to see a turn and re-evaluate based on the next card and my opponent's action.

On the turn, the 9 of spades is added to a relatively dry board. As the defender from the big blind with pocket fives, I am currently in check-call mode. I do not want to bet into the pre-flop aggressor here as it would likely only be called by hands that beat me. Checking allows me to see how Flashlight reacts to the turn card. If they check back, I get a free river and potentially a showdown. If they bet, I will have to evaluate the sizing and my perceived equity against their range.

The turn card paired my nine, giving me middle pair. Given that the opponent called my continuation bet on the flop, they likely have a marginal hand, a draw, or perhaps a queen. Betting here might isolate me against stronger hands that will call, while checking behind allows me to control the pot size and see the river for free, maintaining flexibility for the final street.

With pocket fives on a board of 2c-Qh-7s-9s-Jd, I am facing several overcards. Flashlight checked back on the turn after a continuation bet on the flop, which often indicates a hand with some showdown value but not enough to bet for three streets, or a total air hand. The Jack on the river is a difficult card for me as it connects with many hands in a button opening range. I will check to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or if Flashlight decides to bluff with a missed draw.

I have a pair of nines, which is a marginal showdown hand. Since the opponent checked on the river, there is no need to bet; my pair is likely too weak to extract value from better hands, and betting would only fold out worse hands. Checking behind allows me to reach showdown for free, which is the most optimal way to play this specific holding given the board texture.
K2o is a weak hand with significant reverse implied odds if a King hits. Being first to act on the button, it is best to fold and wait for better starting cards.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h6h. Since the Button has folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind (Flashlight). 8h6h is a solid hand to play in this position due to its semi-connectedness and flush potential. Raising is generally more effective than completing the blind to take initiative and win the pot immediately if the BB folds. I will raise to 300, which is a standard open from the Small Blind.

Holding K5 offsuit in the big blind against an open raise from the small blind, the hand is fundamentally weak and dominated by most of the opponent's raising range. Playing out of position with such a mediocre hand is negative expectation long-term, despite the decent pot odds. Given the tournament depth and the desire to avoid difficult spots with a weak holding, folding is the disciplined choice.

I have Ace-King offsuit on the button, which is a premium starting hand in three-handed play. My goal is to raise for value and leverage my positional advantage. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is consistent with my established opening range and sizing in previous hands, putting pressure on the blinds.

I hold Ace of Diamonds, Five of Diamonds (A5s) in the Small Blind against a button raise. A5s is a strong hand that plays well heads-up and has good equity. Given Flash's wide opening range on the button, I want to take the initiative and prevent the Big Blind from getting a cheap realization of equity. By 3-betting, I can take control of the pot and potentially take down the hand pre-flop, or play a larger pot with initiative. A 3-bet to 900 puts pressure on Flash.

I am facing an open raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind. My hand is J6 offsuit, which is extremely weak and completely unplayable, especially facing this much aggression. Easy fold.

I hold Ace-King offsuit, which is a premium hand, especially in three-handed play. After my initial opening raise from the button, Flashlight 3-bet to 900 from the small blind. AKo is typically ahead of a 3-betting range in this dynamic. I will 4-bet to 2200 to extract value and exert pressure, as this hand is too strong to simply call and allows me to take control of the pot. If Flashlight pushes all-in, I am prepared to call given the short-handed nature of the game and the strength of my hand.

Flash's 4-bet to 2200 indicates significant strength, but with A5s, I have decent equity against many of their holdings. While playing out of position in a 4-bet pot is challenging, the pot odds of 1300 to win 3400 are attractive enough to justify calling. I will see the flop and re-evaluate my position based on the texture.

The flop of 2d 7d 9s is excellent for my holding of Ad 5d, giving me the nut flush draw. Being out of position in a 4-bet pot, the standard strategic play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Flash). This allows me to gauge their strength and keep the pot size manageable while retaining my equity. I am looking to continue on almost any turn card that brings a diamond or improves my hand, but for now, checking is the most disciplined and effective approach.

I hold Ace-King offsuit in a 4-bet pot on a low, relatively dry board [2d 7d 9s]. My opponent, Flashlight, checked to me. Given the low Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) of approximately 1.4, any significant bet here largely commits me to the pot. While I have the range advantage as the 4-better, this board doesn't particularly favor my high-card range. Flashlight's 4-bet calling range likely consists of pocket pairs (JJ-99) or high-suited connectors/broadways. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, realize my equity with two overcards, and avoid being check-raised off my hand. I can re-evaluate on the turn.

The board is paired, reducing the immediate strength of my flush draw, and the pot is a 4-bet pot. The opponent checked the flop, showing some passivity or lack of top-pair strength. I am out of position. Checking again allows me to control the pot size and hopefully realize my equity on the river cheaply. If a diamond hits on the river, I have significant strength; if not, I can re-evaluate. Betting into a paired board as a bluff or semi-bluff is risky if the opponent is trapping, so checking is the most prudent play.

I hold Ace-King offsuit on a board that has not improved my hand. After 4-betting pre-flop and checking the flop for pot control, the turn is a seven, pairing the board. My opponent has checked for a second time. While I could attempt a delayed continuation bet to represent a strong overpair, Flashlight's 4-bet calling range often includes pocket pairs (like 88-JJ) that may not fold to a single bet on this texture. By checking back, I ensure I see the river for free, preserving my stack and maintaining the opportunity to realize my equity if an Ace or King appears, or to evaluate a potential bluff or showdown on the river.

The board is 2d 7d 9s 7h 6h, and I have missed my flush draw with Ad 5d. My opponent has checked behind twice on the flop and turn, which suggests they might have some showdown value like a pair, but are being cautious in a 4-bet pot. My Ace-high is unlikely to win at showdown, and betting would be a high-risk bluff against a range that likely includes at least a pair or a missed overpair that might call a thin bet. Given the 4-bet pre-flop, they are unlikely to fold a pair. Checking is the most disciplined play to minimize further losses.

I hold Ace-King high in a 4-bet pot. The board is 2d-7d-9s-7h-6h. After 4-betting pre-flop, the action has been checked through on every street. My opponent, Flashlight, has checked to me on the river. While I could attempt a bluff to represent an overpair like AA or KK, my hand has significant showdown value against Flashlight's potential range of missed broadway cards (like AQ or AJ) or smaller suited connectors that didn't connect. A bet is unlikely to be called by a worse hand, and it's uncertain if Flashlight would fold a mid-pair like 88, TT, or JJ given the way the hand played out. Therefore, checking back to realize my showdown value is the most prudent play.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with King-Five suited. This is a strong position to open, and the game has been quite passive, with many hands being checked down to the river. Raising to 250 (2.5x the big blind) is a standard opening size that allows me to potentially win the blinds immediately or take control of the hand in position.

I am in the Small Blind with 6s3s, facing a raise from the Button. My hand is very weak and suitedness doesn't compensate for the gap and low ranks. Playing this out of position against a pre-flop raiser is a long-term losing play. The clear decision is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding Jack-Four offsuit. Flashlight raised from the Button to 250. While I am getting decent pot odds (150 to call into a pot of 400), Jack-Four offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Given the tournament context and my current stack lead, there is no need to defend such a weak range against a standard raise. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

A6o is a standard open-raise from the button in a 3-handed game. Having an Ace blocks premium hands like AA, AK, and AQ. I will raise to 2.5x (250) to steal the blinds, and can comfortably fold if either opponent decides to 3-bet.

In a 3-handed game, Queen-Ten suited is a very strong hand. The button (Pro) opened with a standard raise to 250. Being in the Small Blind, I want to take control of the hand and potentially end it pre-flop or isolate the button. I will 3-bet to 850, which puts pressure on both the button and the big blind while still keeping the pot manageable given our stack sizes.

My hand (Ten of Diamonds, Trey of Clubs) is extremely weak, especially when facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind after an initial raise from the Button. Continuing with this hand would require calling a significant raise while playing out of position, which is a negative expected value move. I should fold and wait for a better spot to invest chips.

As anticipated in my previous reasoning, my hand (A6o) is strong enough to attempt a steal from the button, but it performs poorly against a 3-bet range. Flash's 3-bet indicates strength, and I am easily dominated by better Aces or pocket pairs. I am in the Big Blind holding 5h 2c, which is a very weak hand. Pro raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Although the pot odds are 2:1, 52o has extremely low equity and poor playability post-flop, especially when out of position. It is better to fold and wait for a stronger hand than to defend with such a weak holding.

Jack-Nine suited is a strong speculative hand that plays very well from the Button. By opening to 2.5x the big blind, I can apply pressure to steal the blinds and, if called, I will have position post-flop with a hand that has good straight and flush potential.

I am holding Ace-Four suited in the Small Blind, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. The Button (Pro) raised to 250. Given that Button opening ranges are typically wide, and I have the chip lead, I will put in a 3-bet to 850. This exerts pressure on the opener and the Big Blind, allows me to take initiative, and potentially win the pot immediately without seeing a flop out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (T5 offsuit). There has been an opening raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind. Calling here would require investing 750 chips into a bloated pot with a hand that has very poor equity and will be playing out of position against two aggressive opponents. This is an easy fold to preserve my stack.

Facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind, J9s is a bit too loose to defend despite having position. The opponent's 3-betting range likely dominates my hand, and calling could lead to difficult post-flop spots where I am dominated by hands like AJ, QJ, or higher suited connectors. Folding is the disciplined and standard play here.

I am on the button with Ace-Queen suited, which is a premium starting hand. I have the chip lead and want to apply pressure to the blinds. My standard opening raise size throughout this tournament has been 3x the big blind (300 chips). I will continue with this sizing to build value with my strong hand and maintain a consistent betting pattern.

I am in the small blind with 5c 8c, which is a weak hand to defend out of position against an opponent who has been opening frequently from the button. With the Big Blind still to act behind me, calling risks a squeeze or playing a difficult pot with a dominated hand. It is safer to fold and wait for a better spot.
With Pocket Queens in the Big Blind facing a Button open, I have a premium hand. I will 3-bet to build the pot and extract value from worse pairs and broadway hands.

I have AdQd on the button and opened to 300. The Big Blind (Pro) has 3-bet to 1000. AdQd is a very strong hand, especially in a 3-handed game. I have the chip lead and position. While I could 4-bet, calling allows me to play a pot in position with a strong hand against a range that likely includes many hands I dominate or am flipping against. This keeps the pot manageable while I have the positional advantage. I will call the 700 more to see a flop.

With an overpair on a paired 6-T-6 flop that also features a flush draw, I want to c-bet to extract value from worse pocket pairs like JJ, 99, 88, top pair hands like AT, and various diamond draws. Betting around 40% of the pot accomplishes this while also denying free equity to random overcards.

I have the nut flush draw and two overcards (Ace and Queen) on a paired board of 6d-Td-6c. Pro 3-bet pre-flop and is now leading with a continuation bet of 800 into a pot of 2050. My hand has significant equity; I have 9 outs to the nut flush plus potentially 6 outs with my overcards. Calling is the most prudent play here as it allows me to see the turn in position while keeping the pot manageable and allowing Pro to continue betting with a wider range of hands that I might eventually beat or outdraw. Raising risks getting shoved on by overpairs or a full house (TT), whereas calling realizes equity efficiently.

The King of diamonds is a very unfavorable card for my hand. It completes the obvious diamond flush draw and pairs many of the overcards in Flash's pre-flop calling range, such as AK, KQ, and KJs. With no diamond in my hand to redraw to a flush, my pocket Queens have been reduced to a marginal bluff-catcher. Betting here will mostly isolate me against hands that beat me, as worse hands will fold while flushes, trips, and Kings will call or raise. Checking is the most prudent play to control the pot size and evaluate Flash's action.

On the turn, the King of Diamonds completes my nut flush (Ace-high). The board is paired (sixes), which introduces the possibility of a full house (KK, TT, KT, or 66), but the nut flush is still a powerhouse hand that must be bet for value in a three-handed game. Pro 3-bet pre-flop and led on the flop but has now checked on the turn card that completes the diamond draw. This check could represent a slowing down with an overpair (like AA) or a King, or it could be a trap with a full house. I will place a value bet of 1,600 (slightly less than half the pot) to extract value from Kings, Aces, or Tens, while keeping the pot size manageable and setting up a potential shove on the river if the board doesn't pair further.

The King of diamonds on the turn is one of the worst possible cards for my hand. It completes the diamond flush draw and pairs many of the overcards that Flash could have called with pre-flop and on the flop (such as AK, KQ, and KJ). Without holding a diamond, I have no redraw to a flush, leaving me with only two outs to improve to a full house, assuming Flash doesn't already have one. Flash's bet of 1600 signals strength, and since my Queens are now reduced to a weak bluff-catcher against a range heavily saturated with Kings and flushes, folding is the most disciplined and profitable long-term decision.

In a 3-handed game, the button is a highly advantageous position. With 5c 6s, while not a premium hand, it is within the range of hands I should be opening to steal the blinds. Raising to 2.5x the big blind is a standard opening size that puts pressure on the small blind and big blind, allowing me to potentially take down the pot pre-flop or navigate post-flop with the advantage of position.
T5o is a very weak offsuit hand. Facing a raise from the button, there is no reason to put chips in the pot out of position. I will easily fold.

In 3-handed play, defending the big blind against a button raise requires a wider range. Facing a 2.5x open, I need approximately 27% equity to justify a call. Q6o has roughly 39% raw equity against a standard 50% button opening range. Although I will be out of position, the price is attractive enough to see a flop, especially since I am the chip leader and can afford to play the hand speculatively.

On a flop of 7s-5s-8d, I have an open-ended straight draw with my 6h. This board texture is generally better for a Big Blind defending range than a Button opening range, as I have many more combinations of two-pair, sets, and straights. I will check to the pre-flop aggressor to see their continuation bet sizing and intention. Given the strength of my draw, I plan to continue in the hand if faced with a bet.

I have bottom pair (fives) and an open-ended straight draw (needs a 4 or 9 for a straight) on a wet board (7s 5s 8d). This is a strong drawing hand with significant equity. Betting here allows me to take control of the hand, potentially fold out better high-card hands, or build a pot in case I hit my straight. Checking is too passive given the strength of my draw.

On a flop of 7s-5s-8d, my 6h gives me an open-ended straight draw, needing a 4 or a 9. This board is quite coordinated and generally hits a Big Blind's defending range well. Flashlight's continuation bet of 350 into a 600 pot (making it 950) gives me immediate pot odds of approximately 27% to call. With 8 outs to the straight, I have about a 17% chance of hitting on the turn and roughly 32% by the river. Given the potential for implied odds if I hit my straight, calling is a standard and profitable play here. I will call to see how the turn develops.

The turn card is a 6d, making the board 7s-5s-8d-6d. This creates a four-card straight (5-6-7-8) on the board. I have a pair of sixes and an open-ended straight draw (any 4 or 9 makes a straight). However, this board is extremely dangerous as my opponent, who raised from the button, can easily have a 9 or a 4 in their range (e.g., 99, T9, 98, 44, A4s). My pair of sixes is weak here, and even if I hit a straight on the river, I must be cautious of higher straights or flushes (two spades and two diamonds are on the board). I will check to the aggressor to see how they react to this highly coordinated card.

On the turn, the board has improved significantly, giving me two pair (fives and sixes). While the board is straight-heavy (any 4 or 9 makes a straight), I currently hold a very strong hand against most single-pair or draw-heavy ranges. Flash checked, which suggests they might be looking for a free card or are hesitant. I will make a value bet of 750 into the 1200 pot to extract value from draws like 9x or smaller pairs, while also charging them to see the river.

The turn card (6d) completes a four-card straight on the board (5-6-7-8). My hand consists of a pair of sixes and an open-ended straight draw (any 4 or 9). However, because the board is so coordinated, any opponent holding a 9 or a 4 already has a straight. Flashlight's bet of 750 into a 1250 pot (making it 2000) on such a dangerous board indicates significant strength or a high-equity draw. My pair of sixes is extremely weak in this context, losing to any 7, 8, 9, 4, or overpair. Chasing a straight that may already be beaten or result in a split pot is not profitable given the board texture and my position. As the chip leader, I will prioritize preserving my stack and fold this marginal hand.

I have 54o on the Button. This is a very weak offsuit connector. Even with position, stealing here is not profitable enough against standard blinds to justify a raise, and it plays too poorly to call. Folding is the best choice.

I am in the Small Blind with 93o after the button folded. This is one of the weakest hands in poker. While it only costs 50 chips to complete, 93o plays very poorly post-flop, especially out of position against an active Big Blind like Flashlight. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my chip lead.

I am on the button with QJ offsuit. This is a very strong opening hand in 3-handed play. My strategy has been to raise 3x from the button and take down the blinds, which has been successful in many previous hands. Raising to 300 is a standard play here to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop or play in position with a solid hand.

I am in the small blind with T-6 offsuit, which is a weak hand. Playing this hand out of position against an aggressive button raiser is negative EV. I will fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Facing a 3x open from the aggressive chip leader on the button, 7s 5s is a marginal suited gapper. While it can sometimes be defended in the big blind, playing out of position with a medium stack makes folding the safer and more standard choice to avoid tricky post-flop spots.

I am in the button position with Ten-Four offsuit. This is a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity against the ranges of my opponents. As a professional, I prioritize playing disciplined poker and avoiding speculative hands that are dominated by most opening or defending ranges. Folding is the clear strategic choice to preserve my stack and wait for a more advantageous situation.

With A9s in the Small Blind and the action folded to me, I have a very strong hand for a Blind vs Blind encounter. Raising to 300 puts pressure on the Big Blind to defend with marginal holdings, and builds the pot with a hand that has great equity and post-flop playability.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 3s, a very weak hand. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. Even though I have the chip lead and am getting decent pot odds (200 to call for a 600 pot), 83o is a garbage hand that plays poorly out of position. I have no reason to defend such a wide range here against a standard raise from a relatively competent opponent. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.

Ten-Six suited is a very marginal hand, even on the Button. Given my stack size and the fact that I don't want to needlessly bloat the pot with a weak holding, folding is the most solid and standard play here.

I am in the Small Blind with 7d 5s. The Button has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Flashlight). The pot is 150 and it is 50 to call. 75 offsuit is a weak hand. Looking at recent history, Flashlight has been relatively active but has folded to pre-flop pressure or folded post-flop on several occasions (Hands #30, #43). However, raising into the Big Blind with 7-5 offsuit is generally unprofitable unless you have a specific read that they are folding too often. Given the stack depths and the hand quality, I will elect to fold and wait for a better spot, especially since I am playing for a long-term goal of 150 hands and want to avoid high-variance, marginal spots early on.

I am on the button with Qc 3c. While suited, the hand is quite weak with a very low kicker. I have a significant chip lead (14,300) compared to Flashlight (8,700) and Pro (6,850). Both opponents have shown they are willing to defend their blinds or 3-bet (Pro in Hand #42 and #27). Although I could open-raise here to steal the blinds, Q3s is at the bottom of a button opening range. I will choose to fold and wait for a stronger hand to apply pressure, as there is no need to risk chips with marginal holdings at this stage of the tournament.

I am in the small blind with a premium hand (AKo) and the button has folded. This presents a prime opportunity to raise and take control of the pot heads-up against the big blind, or take down the blinds immediately. A raise to 300 is standard to apply pressure.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket Queens (Qs Qh), a premium starting hand. The Small Blind raised to 300. I want to build the pot and extract value, as well as define the hand pre-flop. A 3-bet to 900 is a standard sizing that should get called by weaker pairs, broadways, and suited connectors while punishing the wider range the Small Blind might be opening.

Holding AKo in the small blind, I am facing a 3-bet from the big blind. AKo is too strong to fold, and playing a 3-bet pot out of position can be difficult if I just call. A 4-bet is the preferred strategy here to take back control of the initiative, define the opponent's range, and maximize value with a premium holding.

In a blind versus blind situation, ranges are significantly wider. Pocket Queens is a premium hand, and facing a 4-bet, I am ahead of hands like JJ, TT, AK, and AQs that Flashlight could be holding. Calling would leave an awkward stack-to-pot ratio on the flop, making post-flop decisions difficult, especially if an overcard hits. By 5-bet shoving all-in, I maximize fold equity against his bluffs and semi-bluffs, while happily getting it in against his value range for a ~70bb effective stack.

After 4-betting to 2300 with Ace-King offsuit, the opponent has come back with a 5-bet to 6950. With a total stack of 6450, I have already invested 2300 and am pot-committed. Folding a premium hand like AKo against a 5-bet, despite the danger of running into AA or KK, is not viable given the stack-to-pot ratio and the equity AKo holds against the rest of their likely range (such as JJ, QQ, or AK). I will call to put my remaining chips into the pot.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand, 6-3 offsuit. Given the heads-up nature of this match, completing the small blind to see a flop is a possibility, but folding is the more disciplined choice to avoid playing out of position with a marginal hand and to preserve my stack against an aggressive opponent.

I am in the Small Blind/Button in a heads-up situation. My hand, Ten-Trey offsuit, is very weak. While I have position post-flop, T3o does not play well and is generally a fold in most balanced strategies. With deep stacks (over 140 big blinds), there is no need to play such a marginal hand.

I am in the small blind heads-up with King-Ten offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in this format. I am already in for 50 chips, and raising allows me to take control of the hand and put pressure on the big blind. A raise to 300 is a standard size to build a pot or potentially take it down pre-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 3d, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Small Blind/Button. Given the stack depths and the weakness of my hand, there is no reason to defend or bluff here. Folding is the standard and most profitable play.

I am on the button with J9o, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. Historically, the opponent (Flashlight) has been active but foldable. Raising here is standard to put pressure on the big blind and take control of the pot early. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate for this stack depth and hand strength.
In a heads-up scenario, defending the big blind with Queen-high is standard given the wide opening ranges and the 2:1 pot odds offered. I will call to see a flop and evaluate my position.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-high on a King-Jack-3 board. The opponent has checked the flop. My hand has very little equity against a standard opening range that hits this board quite well (Kings, Jacks, broadway cards). Betting here would be a pure bluff into a range that likely connects with this texture. Checking behind is the most prudent play to see the turn for free, hoping to realize my equity or keep the pot small.

On the flop of Kd Jd 3s, I have second pair with a J. This is a solid hand in a heads-up pot but vulnerable to Kx or diamond draws. Flashlight checked to me. A bet here serves to protect my hand against overcards and draws, and it also extracts value from weaker hands like small pairs or draws that might call. I will bet around 60% of the pot to maintain initiative and define where I stand.

The flop texture of King-Jack-3 is highly connected and hits the pre-flop aggressor's range significantly better than mine. With Queen-high and no legitimate draws or backdoor equity, I have very little chance of winning the hand against any pair or better. Calling a continuation bet here would be mathematically incorrect as I am essentially drawing dead against the majority of the opponent's range. Folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind/Button position with a very weak holding of 9-3 offsuit. In heads-up play, while ranges are wider, 9-3o is not a hand worth investing chips into, especially with a stack size that allows for patience. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play here.

I am on the button with 7h 3d, which is a very weak starting hand. While I could attempt to steal the blinds with a raise, Flashlight has shown willingness to defend and play back. Given the stack depths and the poor quality of my hand, the most prudent action is to fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand, 9-4 offsuit. In a heads-up scenario, while I can play a wide range, this hand lacks the playability to continue against a potential raise or to play post-flop comfortably. Folding is the most disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with King-Four offsuit. In a heads-up situation, a King is generally strong enough to open or defend with. Given that Flashlight has been relatively active and our stacks are almost even, I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds immediately.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 7s, which is a weak offsuit hand. While defending the big blind is standard in heads-up play, 97o is at the bottom of my continuing range, especially when facing a 3x open raise from an aggressive opponent. It is better to fold and conserve my stack for a better spot than to play this marginal hand out of position post-flop.

I am in the small blind with 6h 5h, a playable suited connector. The cost to complete the call is only 50 chips to see a flop. Given we are heads-up, I want to see a flop and potentially outplay the opponent with a speculative hand that has good board coverage.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit. The opponent in the Small Blind (Flashlight) has just completed the blind (limped), and I have the option to check and see a free flop or raise. A3o is a decent starting hand in a heads-up situation, but it's not exceptionally strong. Given that Flashlight just limped, checking is a standard and low-variance play to see if I hit an Ace or a straight draw, while keeping the pot small with a hand that has weak kickers. Raising is an option to take the initiative, but checking is perfectly acceptable here.

On this dry board of 2d-7h-3c, I have hit bottom pair with an Ace kicker (3s-Ac). In a limped pot, my hand is currently quite strong against a wide range of hands Flashlight might be holding. I will check here to see how the opponent reacts, allowing them to potentially bluff or to see a cheap turn, maintaining a small pot with a marginal made hand.

I have a gutshot straight draw with my 5h 6h on a 2d 7h 3c board. This is a good opportunity to semi-bluff, applying pressure to Flash who checked the flop. A bet of 125, which is roughly 60% of the pot, should provide enough fold equity while building the pot for when I hit my straight.

In this limped pot, the board of 2d-7h-3c is very dry. I have middle pair (3s) with an Ace kicker (Ac). Flashlight's bet of 125 into a 200 pot (making it 325 total) is a standard continuation or probe bet. Given that I have a piece of the board and an overcard, my hand is too strong to fold to a single bet in a heads-up situation. However, raising would likely only get called by better hands (like a 7, a better 3, or a pocket pair) and fold out bluffs. Calling allows me to keep their bluffing range in and see a turn card cheaply.

On the turn, the board is 2d-7h-3c-Tc. I have middle pair with 3s and an Ace kicker. In a heads-up limped pot, my hand has some showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value, as I would likely only be called by better hands like a 7, a 10, or a higher pocket pair. Checking is the best play here to control the pot size and potentially see a cheap showdown or induce a bluff from weaker hands that might have missed the board.

The turn card (10c) does not improve my hand. I am holding 6-high with a gutshot straight draw, needing a 4. Since the opponent checked, taking a free card is the most efficient way to realize my equity without overcommitting to a bluff against a player who already called my flop bet, which suggests they have some showdown value. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a river card for free.

In this limped pot, I have a pair of Threes with an Ace kicker on a 2-7-3-T-4 board. My hand has some showdown value against missed draws or weaker pairs that Flashlight might have limped with. Flashlight bet the flop but checked the turn, which often indicates a marginal hand or a give-up on a bluff. Checking the river is the most prudent move; betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands and only serves to build a pot against hands that have me beat. If Flashlight checks behind, I may win the pot with my pair of Threes.

I have successfully made a straight (3-4-5-6-7) with my 5h 6h on the 2d 7h 3c Tc 4h board. Since Flash has checked the river, I have the best hand and must look to extract value. A bet of 250 (roughly half the pot) is appropriate to induce a call from any pair or a ten that Flash might be holding.

In this limped pot, I have a pair of Threes (bottom pair on the flop, now second-to-bottom pair on the river) with an Ace kicker. The action went: check-check pre-flop, I check-called a bet on the flop, we both checked the turn, and now Flashlight is betting 250 into a 450 pot on the river. Flashlight's check on the turn often indicates a lack of a strong hand like a Ten or a high Seven. The river Four is unlikely to have significantly helped most of their range unless they specifically have a Four. Given the pot odds (250 to win a total pot of 950, requiring me to be right roughly 26% of the time), my pair of Threes functions as a reasonable bluff catcher against missed high cards or smaller pairs that Flashlight might be turning into a bluff. I will call to see the showdown.
I have pocket sixes on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a strong starting hand that is well ahead of the average range. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and build a pot.

Facing a standard button raise of 300 from the small blind, my hand 8h 9s is a reasonable defending candidate in the big blind. With 2:1 pot odds and deep stack depth, calling is the most standard and profitable play to realize equity post-flop rather than folding or over-committing with a bluff raise.

The board texture of K-K-2 is extremely favorable for the pre-flop aggressor's range, as Kings are well within a button/small blind opening range. Holding 9-8 high, I have virtually no equity here. Checking is the standard play to preserve stack size, as betting would be a thin bluff with no fold equity against this board texture. I will look to fold to any significant bet from my opponent.

I raised pre-flop with pocket sixes and was called by Flashlight. The flop comes K-K-2, which is a very dry board. While there are two Kings on the board, making it less likely that Flashlight has one, it's also a board where I don't have much equity if I am behind. However, as the pre-flop aggressor, I should typically continuation bet this texture to represent a King or an Ace-high that is currently ahead. Since Flashlight checked to me, a small bet of 200 (1/3 pot) is an effective way to pick up the pot now or define where I stand without overcommitting.

My hand, 8-9 high, has zero equity on a K-K-2 board. The opponent has bet into me, and while the bet size is relatively small (200 into an 800 pot), calling with high-card junk against a board that hits the pre-flop aggressor's range extremely well is a negative expectation play. I have no path to winning the pot, and calling would simply be burning chips. Folding is the disciplined choice here.

I am in the small blind with King-Deuce offsuit. Given the heads-up situation and the pot odds, completing the small blind is the most efficient play to see a flop cheaply and maintain control of the pot.

I have a strong suited broadway hand (KJs) in the Big Blind. Flashlight limped from the button, which often signals a wide or weak range. I want to raise for value and to take initiative in the hand. A raise to 400 (4x the big blind) is a standard play here to build the pot.

I am holding King-Deuce offsuit in the small blind against a raise from the big blind. K2o is a weak hand with poor playability, and facing a raise to 400 (4x the big blind) makes it mathematically disadvantageous to defend. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my stack and avoid playing a marginal hand in a bloated pot.

In a heads-up situation, Q7o is a strong enough hand to open from the button. I want to maintain aggression and put pressure on the big blind. I will raise to 3x the big blind, which has been my standard opening size.

I hold pocket Aces, which is the strongest starting hand. Since my opponent has opened from the button, I need to 3-bet to build the pot and take the initiative rather than just calling. Raising to 1000 is a standard and effective size to generate value and define my opponent's range.

I raised to 300 from the button with Queen-Seven offsuit, and Flashlight responded with a 3-bet to 1000. While I have position for the rest of the hand, Q7o is a marginal hand that performs poorly against a typical 3-betting range. Since we are early in the heads-up match and stacks are nearly even, I prefer to fold and wait for a better spot rather than calling 700 more to play a bloated pot with a weak, easily dominated hand.

I am in the small blind/button position with 7-2 offsuit. While this is a weak hand, in a heads-up scenario, playing the button allows me to see a flop with positional advantage for a small price (50 chips to a 100 chip bet). Given the blinds structure and the fact that I have already invested 50, calling 50 more to see the flop is a standard play to keep the initiative and navigate post-flop.

Flashlight has limped from the button. While Qd2d is a suited hand with a high card, it's not strong enough to consistently raise for value out of position against a limp. Checking allows me to see a flop for free and realize my equity with a hand that plays reasonably well in small, multi-street scenarios if I hit a pair or a draw.

I have Queen-high with a backdoor diamond draw on a paired board (4c 9c 9h). In a limped pot, there's no reason to lead out with a bet here. Checking is the most standard play, allowing me to see how the opponent reacts and potentially see a free turn card to improve my hand or realize my showdown value.

I am holding 2c 7h on a flop of 4c 9c 9h. My hand has virtually no equity, no pair, and no realistic draw. The opponent checked to me, so I can check behind to control the pot size and avoid putting chips in with a hand that has zero showdown value. Betting here as a bluff is risky on a paired board where the opponent might easily have a piece of the board or a better hand, so checking is the most strategic play.

In a limped pot, the Ace on the turn is a significant card. Since both players checked the flop, the ranges are wide and likely weak. I have Queen-high with no draw. While the Ace is a good card to bluff, I prefer checking to see if Flashlight checks back, allowing me to potentially reach a cheap showdown or evaluate a river action. If Flashlight bets, I will likely fold as I have no equity against an Ace or a Nine.

The board is 4c 9c 9h Ac and I hold 2c 7h. I have virtually no showdown value, and my only 'draw' is a runner-runner flush (as I hold the 2c). Since the opponent has checked twice on this board, they may be trapping or just weak, but betting with 7-high into a pot where my opponent has shown potential strength or patience is not a high-percentage move. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size and see a river card for free without risking more chips on a weak hand.

The board is double-paired with Aces and Nines (4c 9c 9h Ac As). My best five-card hand is now two pair, Aces and Nines, with a Queen kicker (As-Ac-9c-9h-Qd). Given that both players have checked every street in a limped pot, it is highly unlikely that either of us has an Ace or a Nine. Most of Flashlight's range here consists of weak high-card hands or small pocket pairs that are now also playing the board's two pair. A bet is unlikely to be called by a worse hand (like Jack-high or lower), and I have decent showdown value with a Queen kicker. Checking is the most sensible action to reach showdown for free.

The board is As Ac 9h 9c 4c. I hold 7h 2c. My best 5-card hand is As Ac 9h 9c 7h. The opponent checked, and there is essentially no value in betting here; any hand that calls me would likely beat my Seven-high, and I cannot get value from worse hands. Checking is the standard play to take the showdown, which will likely result in a split pot if the opponent has a worse kicker or a Four, or a loss if they have any card higher than a Seven.
